common_name: Belle of Georgia Peach (Classic White Freestone Heirloom Peach)

planting.note: Plant bare-root trees in late winter while dormant; Belle of Georgia requires approximately 850 chill hours (temperatures below 45°F) and is reliably productive in zones 5–9 where adequate winter chilling accumulates; plant in well-drained, slightly acidic (pH 6.0–6.5), deep fertile soil in full sun; space 15–20 feet apart.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun; Belle of Georgia is a self-fruitful peach requiring no cross-pollinator; thin fruit to 6–8 inches after petal fall; harvest when fruit is fully colored and gives slightly to pressure — white-fleshed peaches like Belle of Georgia lose the green background and develop a creamy-white blush at full ripeness. Belle of Georgia is one of the great classic American peach varieties, an heirloom white-fleshed freestone that has been grown in Southern home orchards for over 150 years, originating in Georgia before 1870 from a cross believed to involve Chinese Cling and other Southern peach stock. The variety demonstrates a characteristic that distinguishes white-fleshed peaches from the more common yellow-fleshed types: the flesh is pure white to blush-pink, with a flavor that is typically sweeter and less acidic than yellow peaches, with floral aromatic notes that many peach connoisseurs consider more complex and refined than yellow varieties. The flavor at peak ripeness — juicy, very sweet, almost honeyed, with a delicate fragrance — is exceptional for fresh eating and traditional Southern preserves. The skin is creamy white to pale pink with a bright red blush over much of the surface, making the ripened fruit visually distinctive. Belle of Georgia is a mid-season variety that ripens in late June to mid-July in most of the South, producing large, fully freestone fruits that separate cleanly from the pit at full ripeness. The trees are vigorous and consistent producers with reasonable disease tolerance under good management. A genuine heirloom variety that has earned its continuous popularity over more than a century of Southern home orchard growing.
sources: Penn State Extension — Tree Fruit Production Guide

common_name: Loring Peach (Mid-South Commercial Standard Yellow Freestone Peach)

planting.note: Plant bare-root in late winter in well-drained, slightly acidic, deep fertile soil; Loring requires approximately 750 chill hours, making it adaptable to zones 6–9 and well-suited to the mid-South and upper Gulf South where its chill requirement is reliably met; trees are self-fruitful but produce heavier crops with a nearby companion peach.

directions: Plant in deep, well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic soil in full sun; thin fruit to 6–8 inches after petal fall for maximum fruit size; Loring ripens mid-season (late June to mid-July in zone 7–9) with large, well-colored yellow-fleshed freestone fruit; apply preventive fungicide starting at bud swell for brown rot and peach leaf curl control. Loring peach is one of the most widely planted commercial peach varieties in the mid-South and a benchmark mid-season variety throughout the southeastern United States, developed at the Missouri Agricultural Experiment Station (USDA collaboration) and released in 1944. The variety takes its name from Mount Loring, a traditional Missouri peach-growing area. For decades, Loring was the dominant commercial peach in Tennessee, Missouri, Arkansas, and adjacent southeastern states, praised for its reliable high yields, large fruit size, excellent flavor, and shelf life compared to other varieties of its era. The fruits are large to very large, round to slightly oblate, with a predominantly red skin over a yellow-orange background — very attractive at roadside markets and farm stands. The flesh is yellow, firm, and fully freestone at full maturity, with a well-balanced rich peach flavor that compares favorably with Elberta while generally producing larger, better-colored fruit under mid-South conditions. Loring's moderate chill requirement (approximately 750 hours) fits the climate of the mid-South better than Elberta (which needs 800–1000 hours) in years with insufficient winter cold. It has been partially replaced by newer varieties with improved disease resistance in commercial production, but remains a standard recommendation for home orchardists throughout the Southeast.
sources: University of Tennessee Extension — Peach Cultivar Evaluations
Missouri Agricultural Experiment Station — Loring peach release notes

common_name: Harvester Peach (Mid-South Early Freestone Peach, Gulf South Yellow Freestone)

planting.note: Plant bare-root in late winter in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic (pH 6.0–6.5) deep soil; Harvester requires approximately 750 chill hours, making it suitable for zones 6–9 and well-adapted to the mid-South and upper Gulf South from east Texas through the Carolinas; trees are self-fruitful; early-ripening season makes Harvester a valuable first-of-season peach for markets.

directions: Plant in deep, fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun; thin fruit in April to 6–8 inches; Harvester matures early (late May to mid-June in most of the Gulf South), making it one of the earliest large-fruited peaches; prune annually to open-vase form; good for direct-market sales and home fresh eating — early ripening gives it a market window before the main-season varieties arrive. Harvester is an early-season yellow freestone peach variety developed by North Carolina State University and the USDA (released 1971) that became an important variety for mid-South commercial peach production throughout the Carolinas, Georgia, Tennessee, and the Gulf South for its ability to deliver large, well-flavored, fully freestone yellow peaches earlier in the season than the standard mid-season benchmarks like Elberta and Loring. Early-season peach varieties command premium prices in direct farm markets because consumers are eager for the first fresh peaches of the year, and Harvester's ripening time — typically late May to mid-June in zone 7–9 — puts it among the first significant-sized freestone peaches available in most markets. The fruits are medium to large, round, with an attractive red blush over yellow skin, firm yellow flesh, and a clean freestone pit at full maturity. The chill hour requirement of approximately 750 hours fits the mid-South and Gulf South climate well in most winters, making Harvester a reliable producer under typical zone 7–8 conditions. Trees are vigorous and productive with moderate disease susceptibility requiring a standard spray program for brown rot and other peach diseases. An excellent choice for home orchardists who want the earliest possible fresh peach harvest.
sources: North Carolina State University Extension — Peach Cultivar Evaluation
Mississippi State University Extension — Peach Variety Evaluation

common_name: Madison Peach (Cold-Hardy Virginia Heirloom Freestone Peach)

planting.note: Plant bare-root in late winter in well-drained, slightly acidic, deep, fertile soil; Madison requires approximately 850 chill hours and is one of the most cold-hardy named peach varieties, with flower buds tolerating temperatures down to 0°F or below — significantly more cold-resistant than Elberta and other standard varieties, making it the premier choice for orchards in zones 4–6 where late spring frosts or severe winter cold reduce peach production reliability.

directions: Plant in well-drained, deep, fertile, slightly acidic soil in full sun; Madison is self-fruitful; thin fruit in spring to 6–8 inches; this variety is particularly valuable where late spring frosts endanger developing blossoms — its cold-hardy flower buds and frost resistance make it among the most reliable peach varieties in colder zones. Madison peach was developed at the Virginia Polytechnic Institute and State University (released 1963) as part of a program to develop peach varieties combining high fruit quality with improved cold-hardiness of the flower buds — addressing the primary limitation on peach production in the colder parts of the South and the lower Midwest where late spring frosts can destroy the blossoms of standard varieties like Elberta even in years when winter temperatures are adequate for the trees to survive. The key innovation of Madison is its extraordinary flower bud cold-hardiness: Madison buds have been documented surviving temperatures of -5°F to 0°F with minimal damage in some trials, making it significantly more frost-resilient than Elberta (whose buds are damaged at 28–30°F during bloom). This characteristic translates to reliable cropping in zones 4–6 where other varieties fail every few years due to late frost blossom kill. The fruits are medium to large, red-blushed over golden-yellow skin, with firm, yellow, fully freestone flesh and a well-flavored, balanced sweet-tart peach quality. Madison ripens in mid-season (late July to mid-August in its Virginia homeland, somewhat earlier in warmer zones). It remains one of the most important variety recommendations for cold-region home orchardists in the Mid-Atlantic, Appalachian, and upper Midwest states.
sources: Virginia Polytechnic Institute — Madison peach release
Penn State Extension — Tree Fruit Production Guide

common_name: Reliance Peach (Ultra-Cold-Hardy Northern Peach, New Hampshire Freestone)

planting.note: Plant bare-root in early spring in well-drained, slightly acidic, fertile, deep soil; Reliance requires approximately 850 chill hours and is one of the most cold-hardy peach varieties available, with dormant wood hardy to -25°F and flower buds among the most cold-tolerant of any named variety — the premier peach for zones 4–6 where winter hardiness and late-frost resilience are paramount; self-fruitful.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic soil; thin to 6–8 inches in spring; Reliance is self-fruitful and requires no cross-pollinator; the trees are vigorous and productive, delivering reliable crops in zones where other peach varieties fail due to winter cold; fruit ripens early-mid season (late July to mid-August in zones 4–6). Reliance peach is the most cold-hardy named peach variety in widespread cultivation in North America, developed by F.H. Emerson at the University of New Hampshire (released 1964) specifically for the challenging peach-growing conditions of New England and the northern United States where standard peach varieties like Elberta, Loring, and Madison lose their flower buds to winter cold or late spring frosts too frequently to be reliable crops. The extraordinary cold-hardiness of Reliance is its defining characteristic: dormant wood has survived -25°F in New Hampshire orchard trials, and the flower buds show significantly better cold-tolerance than most other varieties. In comparative cold-hardiness tests, Reliance consistently outperforms all other widely-available named peach varieties, giving it a unique market niche as 'the peach you can actually grow in the North.' Fruit quality is good but not exceptional compared to premium mid-South varieties — the fruits are medium-sized, round to oval, with a red blush over yellow background, and a pleasant yellow-fleshed, sweet, fully freestone flavor. The flavor is somewhat mild compared to full-sun, high-quality southern peaches, reflecting the shorter, cooler northern growing season. However, for gardeners in zones 4–6 where reliable peach production from standard varieties is difficult or impossible, Reliance delivers consistent, acceptable-quality fresh peaches from a tree that simply survives winters that would kill other varieties.
sources: University of New Hampshire Agricultural Experiment Station — Reliance peach release
University of Maine Cooperative Extension — Peach Production
Cornell Cooperative Extension — Tree Fruit Production Guide
